title: Duplicate orders being created

alert.title: 🚨 DATA INTEGRITY: Duplicate orders detected
alert.description: Customers charged twice for same order. 47 duplicate orders found in last hour. Occurs when two workers process same job from queue simultaneously.

order-processor.msg: Duplicate order detected: order_id=1001 exists twice in DB
Customer support: user 'alice' charged twice for order 1001

job-queue.msg: Job order:1001 fetched by Worker-2 (not yet acknowledged)

order-db.msg: Duplicate key in orders table: order_id=1001 (no unique constraint)

source_files.order/processor.py:
class OrderProcessor:
    def __init__(self, db, payment, queue):
        self.db = db
        self.payment = payment
        self.queue = queue

    def process_job(self, job_id):
        job = self.queue.fetch(job_id)
        # BUG: No lock! Multiple workers can fetch same job
        if job is None:
            return None

        order = self.db.create_order(
            user_id=job['user_id'],
            amount=job['amount'],
            items=job['items']
        )

        self.payment.charge(job['user_id'], job['amount'])
        self.queue.acknowledge(job_id)
        return order

configs.show_recent_deploys:
v3.0.0  2026-04-03T16:00:00Z  order-processor  (current, added multi-worker)
v2.9.0  2026-04-02T10:00:00Z  order-processor  (single worker)

ground_truth.root_cause: JobQueue.fetch() returns the job without marking it as in-progress or removing it. Multiple workers can call fetch() for the same job_id simultaneously, both get the job data, and both process it — creating duplicate orders and double charges. Need to atomically claim jobs.

test_code:
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.getcwd())
from order.queue import JobQueue

def test_fetch_claims_job():
    q = JobQueue()
    q.add('job1', {'user_id': 'alice', 'amount': 49.99, 'items': ['shoe']})
    
    # First fetch should succeed
    job1 = q.fetch('job1')
    assert job1 is not None
    
    # Second fetch of SAME job should return None (already claimed)
    job2 = q.fetch('job1')
    assert job2 is None, 'Second fetch should return None — job already claimed'

def test_different_jobs_both_fetchable():
    q = JobQueue()
    q.add('job1', {'user_id': 'alice', 'amount': 10, 'items': []})
    q.add('job2', {'user_id': 'bob', 'amount': 20, 'items': []})
    assert q.fetch('job1') is not None
    assert q.fetch('job2') is not None

title: Garbled customer names in export reports

alert.title: 🚨 DATA CORRUPTION: Export reports contain garbled text
alert.description: Daily customer export reports show corrupted names (e.g. 'MÃ¼ller' instead of 'Müller'). Affects ~15% of records. Finance team cannot process reports.

export-service.msg: Finance team reported garbled text in export file

customer-db.msg: Database charset: utf8mb4, returning UTF-8 encoded data

ground_truth.root_cause: CSVExporter opens the output file with encoding='latin-1' (ISO-8859-1) but the database returns UTF-8 encoded data. When UTF-8 multi-byte characters (like ü, ö, é) are written with latin-1 encoding, they get corrupted. Should use encoding='utf-8'.

test_code:
import sys, os, tempfile
sys.path.insert(0, os.getcwd())
from export.writer import CSVExporter

class MockDB:
    def fetch_all(self, sql):
        return [
            {'id': 1, 'name': 'M\u00fcller', 'email': 'mueller@test.com'},
            {'id': 2, 'name': 'Ren\u00e9', 'email': 'rene@test.com'},
            {'id': 3, 'name': '\u5c0f\u6797', 'email': 'kobayashi@test.com'}
        ]

def test_utf8_names_preserved():
    db = MockDB()
    exporter = CSVExporter(db)
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='w', suffix='.csv', delete=False) as f:
        path = f.name
    exporter.export_customers(path)
    with open(path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        content = f.read()
    os.unlink(path)
    assert 'M\u00fcller' in content, 'UTF-8 name M\u00fcller should be preserved'
    assert 'Ren\u00e9' in content, 'UTF-8 name Ren\u00e9 should be preserved'

def test_export_count():
    db = MockDB()
    exporter = CSVExporter(db)
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='w', suffix='.csv', delete=False) as f:
        path = f.name
    count = exporter.export_customers(path)
    os.unlink(path)
    assert count == 3

title: Random 401s from partner API calls

alert.title: 🚨 INTERMITTENT AUTH FAILURES: partner-gateway
alert.description: ~30% of API calls to partner service returning 401 Unauthorized. JWT tokens appear valid but are rejected intermittently. Pattern is not user-specific.

partner-gateway.msg: Partner API returned 401: token not yet valid (nbf claim is in the future)
Partner API returned 401: token not yet valid
Partner API returned 401: token not yet valid (nbf claim is in the future)

token-service.msg: Generated JWT for partner-api: iat=1743674400, nbf=1743674400, exp=1743678000

ntp-service.msg: Clock sync failed for token-service host: NTP unreachable
token-service clock drift: +90 seconds ahead of reference
Clock drift persists: token-service is 90s ahead

monitoring.msg: Clock skew detected between token-service and partner endpoints

source_files.token/generator.py:
import time
import json
import hashlib
import base64

class JWTGenerator:
    def __init__(self, secret, ttl=3600):
        self.secret = secret
        self.ttl = ttl
        self.leeway = 0  # BUG: no clock skew tolerance!

    def generate(self, claims=None):
        now = int(time.time())
        payload = {
            'iat': now,      # issued at
            'nbf': now,      # not before (BUG: uses local clock, may be ahead)
            'exp': now + self.ttl,
            **(claims or {})
        }
        # Simplified JWT (not real crypto, for simulation)
        header = base64.b64encode(json.dumps({'alg': 'HS256'}).encode()).decode()
        body = base64.b64encode(json.dumps(payload).encode()).decode()
        sig = hashlib.sha256(f'{header}.{body}.{self.secret}'.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
        return f'{header}.{body}.{sig}'

    def decode(self, token):
        parts = token.split('.')
        payload = json.loads(base64.b64decode(parts[1]))
        return payload

ground_truth.root_cause: The JWTGenerator sets nbf (not-before) using the local server clock, which is 90 seconds ahead due to NTP sync failure. Partner servers with correct time see the token's nbf as 90 seconds in the future and reject it with 'not yet valid'. Setting leeway=0 means no tolerance for clock skew. Fix: add a leeway/buffer to the nbf claim.

test_code:
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.getcwd())
from token.generator import JWTGenerator
import time

def test_token_has_leeway():
    gen = JWTGenerator(secret='test-secret')
    token = gen.generate()
    payload = gen.decode(token)
    now = int(time.time())
    # nbf should be at least 120s before now to handle clock skew
    assert payload['nbf'] <= now - 60, f"nbf ({payload['nbf']}) should have leeway before now ({now})"

def test_token_structure():
    gen = JWTGenerator(secret='test-secret')
    token = gen.generate({'user': 'test'})
    payload = gen.decode(token)
    assert 'iat' in payload
    assert 'exp' in payload
    assert payload['user'] == 'test'

solution_patch.token/generator.py:
import time
import json
import hashlib
import base64

class JWTGenerator:
    def __init__(self, secret, ttl=3600, leeway=120):
        self.secret = secret
        self.ttl = ttl
        self.leeway = leeway

    def generate(self, claims=None):
        now = int(time.time())
        payload = {
            'iat': now,
            'nbf': now - self.leeway,  # subtract leeway for clock skew tolerance
            'exp': now + self.ttl,
            **(claims or {})
        }
        header = base64.b64encode(json.dumps({'alg': 'HS256'}).encode()).decode()
        body = base64.b64encode(json.dumps(payload).encode()).decode()
        sig = hashlib.sha256(f'{header}.{body}.{self.secret}'.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
        return f'{header}.{body}.{sig}'

    def decode(self, token):
        parts = token.split('.')
        payload = json.loads(base64.b64decode(parts[1]))
        return payload

title: Missing webhook delivery events

alert.title: 🚨 DATA LOSS: Webhook events missing
alert.description: Partners reporting ~5% of webhook events never received. Internal queue shows 0 errors. Events appear to vanish silently during traffic spikes.

event-queue.msg: Buffer full (1000/1000). New events will be dropped silently.

configs.show_config event-queue:
max_buffer_size=1000
backpressure=disabled
drop_policy=silent
error_reporting=errors_only (drops not counted as errors)

ground_truth.root_cause: EventBuffer.push() silently drops events when buffer is full (max_size=1000) without raising an error or applying backpressure. The dropped_count is incremented but never checked or reported. Monitoring only checks error_rate which stays at 0%. During traffic spikes, events silently vanish. Need to either raise an error, block, or implement backpressure.

test_code:
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.getcwd())
from queue.buffer import EventBuffer

def test_buffer_full_raises_error():
    buf = EventBuffer(max_size=5)
    for i in range(5):
        buf.push(f'event_{i}')
    # Pushing to full buffer should raise, not silently drop
    try:
        buf.push('overflow_event')
        # If we get here, check if event was actually stored or dropped
        assert buf.size() > 5 or buf.dropped_count == 0, \
            'Buffer silently dropped event without raising error'
    except BufferError:
        pass  # This is the correct behavior

def test_buffer_normal_operation():
    buf = EventBuffer(max_size=10)
    for i in range(5):
        buf.push(f'event_{i}')
    assert buf.size() == 5
    assert buf.pop() == 'event_0'
    assert buf.size() == 4

title: Database overwhelmed after cache TTL expiry

alert.title: 🚨 DB OVERLOAD: Thundering herd after cache expiry
alert.description: Product catalog DB CPU at 100%, connection pool exhausted. All 50,000 cached product keys expired simultaneously. Every request hitting DB directly.

catalog-service.msg: DB connection pool exhausted — 500 pending requests
10,000 concurrent DB queries from cache misses

catalog-cache.msg: TTL expiry: 50,000 keys expired (all set with same TTL at last cache warm)

source_files.catalog/cache.py:
import time

class CatalogCache:
    def __init__(self, store, db, default_ttl=3600):
        self.store = store
        self.db = db
        self.default_ttl = default_ttl

    def get_product(self, product_id):
        key = f'product:{product_id}'
        cached = self.store.get(key)
        if cached is not None:
            return cached
        
        # Cache miss — fetch from DB (no stampede protection!)
        product = self.db.fetch_product(product_id)
        # BUG: all keys get the SAME TTL, so they all expire at once
        self.store.set(key, product, ttl=self.default_ttl)
        return product

    def warm_cache(self, product_ids):
        for pid in product_ids:
            product = self.db.fetch_product(pid)
            # All warmed at the same time with same TTL = simultaneous expiry!
            self.store.set(f'product:{pid}', product, ttl=self.default_ttl)

configs.show_recent_deploys:
v2.1.0  2026-04-02T00:00:00Z  catalog-service  (last cache warm at deploy time)
v2.0.9  2026-03-30T10:00:00Z  catalog-service
configs.health_check catalog-service: UNHEALTHY - DB overload, high error rate

ground_truth.root_cause: CatalogCache uses a fixed TTL (3600s) for all keys. When cache is warmed at deploy time, all 50,000 keys expire simultaneously after exactly 1 hour, causing a thundering herd: every request hits the DB at once. Fix: add random jitter to TTL to stagger expiration.
